narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 54% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. The primary driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing around the 5th or 6th decile. This low ranking carried the most weight in the model's decision, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong performance through later undergraduate years. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's close to the average of 3.21 seen in students who typically pass the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score of 30 provided some helpful counterbalance, nudging the odds toward passing, though it's notably below the dataset average of about 37. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and plan to attend full-time—which is the norm for over 90% of students—played lesser roles. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile stands out for that weaker Year 3 performance, overshadowing your solid GPA and despite your full-time commitment. While factors like your background as an Asian male weren't direct drivers here, they fit within broader patterns the model considers. This rejection doesn't define your potential; it just highlights where the model saw risks based on historical data. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic decile through targeted study habits or retaking key assessments, and consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above average scores. Building a stronger narrative around your Year 3 improvement could also help in reapplying or appealing to other schools. With dedication, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
